ppl: Perplexity is computed using GPT-2 (small) as the external scorer, matching MarkLLM's Table 4 setup. PPL is reported for C4 only. WMT16 PPL values were collected but omitted from this table because GPT-2 (an English-trained model) assigns high perplexity to fluent German continuations on grounds of language-mismatch rather than quality, conflating cross-lingual model behavior with watermark cost. Additionally, Unigram on C4 produces a counterintuitive negative ΔPPL: matched-prompt inspection showed watermarked text is not visibly degraded, so the negative delta likely reflects overlap between Unigram's fixed green list and GPT-2's high-probability tokens, biasing the scorer in Unigram's favor. PPL deltas should be interpreted with this scorer-prior interaction in mind.
log_diversity: Log-diversity is reported with two aggregation choices. LogDiv_corpus pools n-grams (n=2,3,4) across all 100 texts per (combo, variant), capturing cross-text variety. LogDiv_pertext computes log-diversity per sample and averages, capturing within-text repetition. Both aggregations agree directionally (wm > uwm across nearly all combos) but answer different questions. Absolute values differ from MarkLLM Table 4's reported log-diversity (~7–9) due to differing aggregation; within-study deltas remain valid.
scale_floor_125m: OPT-125M log-diversity values are visibly inflated relative to OPT-1.3B and OPT-2.7B, particularly on WMT16. Diagnostic inspection showed OPT-125M produces partially-degenerate text on cross-lingual prompts (verbatim prompt repetition followed by topic-drifting sentences with little semantic coherence). N-gram diversity metrics reward this novelty without measuring quality. Comparative claims across model scale should focus on the 1.3B → 2.7B contrast, where both models produce coherent text.
